← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Mediterranean

Greek and Roman antiquity — the classical forms that Europe keeps returning to.

The classical world's obsession with the human body, proportion, and idealized form shaped every European revival that came after — Renaissance, Neoclassical, and on. Much of it survives only in fragments.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Palace of Knossos
Minoan · c. 1900 BCE · Crete
A sprawling Bronze Age palace with running water, frescoes of dolphins and bull-leapers, and a storage maze that may be the origin of the Minotaur myth.
Kamares Ware
Middle Minoan · c. 1800 BCE · Crete
Thin-walled polychrome pottery painted with rippling tendrils and marine forms on a glossy black ground. Named after the sacred cave on Mount Ida where the first examples were found tossed as offerings.
Phaistos Disc
Minoan · c. 1700 BCE · Crete
A fired clay disk stamped with 241 signs in a spiral. Still undeciphered. One of the earliest known uses of movable type — by more than 2,500 years.
Snake Goddess
Minoan faience · c. 1600 BCE · Knossos
A bare-breasted figurine gripping writhing snakes, her flounced skirt falling in tight registers, a feline perched on her head. Found in the palace 'Temple Repository' pits at Knossos, she is probably a household deity or priestess rather than a goddess.
Akrotiri Frescoes
Cycladic/Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Thera (Santorini)
A Bronze Age town buried by the Thera eruption in meters of ash — its plastered rooms preserved images of saffron-picking girls, fishermen, and a miniature harbor fleet. When excavation began in 1967 the colors were still bright.
Prince of the Lilies
Minoan · c. 1600 BCE · Knossos
A reconstructed relief fresco of a figure crowned with peacock-feathered tiara striding through a field of lilies. Arthur Evans combined three scattered plaster fragments into his imagined 'Priest-King' — later scholars think the pieces likely belong to different figures entirely.
Mask of Agamemnon
Mycenaean · c. 1550 BCE
A beaten-gold funerary mask from Grave Circle A at Mycenae. Schliemann announced he had "looked upon the face of Agamemnon" — though this mask is 300 years too early.
Bull-Leaping Fresco
Minoan · c. 1500 BCE · Knossos
Three figures vault over a charging bull on the upper east wing of Knossos: the dark-skinned leaper mid-somersault, pale women stationed before and behind. Whether ritual, sport, or myth, it remains the iconic image of Minoan Crete.
Obelisk of Theodosius
Egyptian carved 15th c. BCE, re-erected 390 CE · Istanbul
An Egyptian obelisk Thutmose III set up at Karnak; Theodosius hauled it to Constantinople and planted it on a marble base carved with Byzantine court scenes. Two monuments in one.
Hagia Triada Sarcophagus
Late Minoan · c. 1400 BCE · Crete
A painted limestone sarcophagus showing funeral rites on all four sides — a bull sacrifice, women carrying pails of blood, the deceased receiving offerings. Our clearest window onto Minoan ritual.
Treasury of Atreus
Mycenaean · c. 1250 BCE · Mycenae
A tholos tomb — a stone beehive thirteen meters high, held up only by its own corbelled weight. For fifteen centuries, the largest dome in the world.

1000 BCE – 0

Dipylon Amphora
Geometric Greek · c. 750 BCE
A grave marker nearly five feet tall, painted with stylized mourners in a prothesis scene. The moment figurative imagery returns to Greek pottery after five centuries of pure geometry.
New York Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 590 BCE
One of the earliest freestanding Greek marble nudes, Attic. Still stiff with Egyptian influence — striding left foot, clenched fists — but the archaic smile has arrived.
François Vase
by Kleitias & Ergotimos
Attic black-figure · c. 570 BCE · signed Kleitias/Ergotimos
A volute krater dense with 270 named figures in six stacked registers — Achilles ambushing Troilus, the marriage of Peleus and Thetis, the Calydonian boar hunt. Found in fragments in an Etruscan tomb near Chiusi in 1844.
Moschophoros (Calf Bearer)
Archaic Greek · c. 560 BCE · Acropolis
A man carries a calf to sacrifice; both smile — the archaic grin. His tunic is incised rather than carved, an early convention already on its way out.
Kroisos Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 530 BCE
A young man stepping forward, hands at his sides, slight smile — the archaic formula. Found on a grave at Anavyssos, one leg at a time.
Sarcophagus of the Spouses
Etruscan · c. 520 BCE · Italy
A painted terracotta couple reclining together on their tomb lid — smiling, touching, treated as equals. Unthinkable in contemporary Greek or Egyptian funerary art.
Euphronios Krater
by Euphronios
Archaic Greek · Euphronios &amp; Euxitheos · c. 515 BCE
A red-figure calyx-krater showing Hypnos and Thanatos carrying Sarpedon's body. Looted from an Etruscan tomb; caught up in a 30-year dispute; finally returned to Italy in 2008.
Apollo of Veii
by attributed to Vulca
Etruscan · c. 510 BCE · Veii
A full-size polychrome terracotta from the roofline of the Portonaccio sanctuary, striding forward to contest Herakles for a stolen deer. Its broad smile and swinging drapery put Etruscan sculpture at parity with anything being made in archaic Greece.
Temple of Aphaia
Late Archaic Greek · c. 500 BCE · Aegina
A limestone temple whose pedimental sculptures, now in Munich, capture the hinge between Archaic stiffness and Classical naturalism — look at the east and west together to see a style changing.
Roman Forum
Roman Republic → Imperial · 7th c. BCE – 4th c. CE
The civic heart of ancient Rome — law courts, speaker's platforms, temples, triumphal arches, all crammed into a low strip between the Palatine and Capitoline hills.
Kritios Boy
Early Classical Greek · c. 480 BCE
The hinge between Archaic and Classical — the first marble body where weight shifts onto one leg (contrapposto), giving the figure life.
Tomb of the Leopards
Etruscan painted tomb · c. 480 BCE · Tarquinia
A small banquet chamber whose back wall shows two leopards flanking a lintel, below which men recline on couches while flute and lyre players serenade them. The brilliant red-and-black palette and easy joy have no parallel in surviving Greek painting.
Tomb of the Diver
Greek colonial · c. 480 BCE · Paestum
The only known example of Greek figural tomb painting from the classical period. A young man dives into water beneath a lone tree — possibly a metaphor for the leap into the afterlife; the side walls show a genial symposium underway.
Charioteer of Delphi
Early Classical · c. 478 BCE
One of the few surviving Greek bronzes. His calm expression, inset with glass and copper eyes, is a moment in a victory lap — all force held still.
Ludovisi Throne
Greek · c. 470 BCE
A marble three-sided relief of uncertain function (a throne? an altar?). On its main panel, Aphrodite rising from the sea, draped in a wet gown that might as well not be there.
Temple of Zeus at Olympia
Classical Greek · c. 470 BCE
Once held the chryselephantine Zeus — one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world, carved by Phidias. The temple fell in an earthquake; the Zeus was hauled to Constantinople and burned.
Artemision Bronze
Classical Greek · c. 460 BCE
Pulled from a Roman shipwreck off Cape Artemision. Either Zeus throwing a thunderbolt or Poseidon throwing a trident — his missing weapon makes it impossible to say.
Riace Bronzes
Classical Greek · c. 460–450 BCE · Italy
Two life-size bronze warriors pulled from the sea off Calabria in 1972. Their teeth are silver, their eyelashes hammered copper.
Discobolus
by Myron
Classical Greek · c. 450 BCE (Roman copy)
Myron's discus thrower — the original bronze is lost; only Roman marble copies survive. A moment just before release, coiled and still.
Capitoline Wolf
Etruscan or Medieval bronze · 5th c. BCE or 13th c. CE
The she-wolf nursing Romulus and Remus — emblem of Rome. Once thought ancient Etruscan; recent analysis suggests a medieval re-casting.
Doryphoros
by Polykleitos
Classical Greek · Polykleitos · c. 440 BCE
The "Spear Bearer" — a treatise on ideal human proportion made in marble. Polykleitos wrote a book called the Canon about it; only this statue survives.
Parthenon Frieze
Classical Greek · Pheidias workshop · c. 440 BCE
A continuous band 160 meters long, showing the Panathenaic procession — horses, musicians, elders, gods receiving the city's tribute. Half survives in the British Museum, half in Athens.
Temple of Poseidon at Sounion
Classical Greek · c. 440 BCE · Attica
White marble columns on a cape where the sea drops 60 meters away on three sides. Byron carved his name into one of the columns in 1810.
The Parthenon
Classical Greek · 447–432 BCE · Athens
Temple to Athena on the Acropolis. The columns lean inward and swell slightly in the middle — optical corrections so they look perfectly straight.
Nike of Paionios
by Paionios
Classical Greek · Paionios · c. 420 BCE · Olympia
The Messenians and Naupactians dedicated her at Olympia after a victory over Sparta. Nike lands on a plinth, wind pressing drapery against her body — 170 years before the Winged Victory of Samothrace.
Erechtheion
Classical Greek · 421–406 BCE · Athens
Temple on the sacred north side of the Acropolis. Six marble maidens (the Caryatids) hold up the porch in place of columns.
Chimera of Arezzo
Etruscan · c. 400 BCE
Bronze of the mythical lion-goat-snake beast, wounded and snarling. Found in 1553 and immediately cleaned up by Cellini; the inscription on its leg is Etruscan, not Latin.
Aphrodite of Knidos
by Praxiteles
Classical Greek · Praxiteles · c. 360 BCE
The first free-standing nude female figure in Greek sculpture — scandalous enough that men climbed her sanctuary walls at night. Only copies survive.
Lady of Elche
Iberian · c. 4th c. BCE · Spain
A limestone bust with enormous spoked headdress-wheels over the ears. Iberian, not Greek — the style belongs to a world that left no writing to explain itself.
Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
Greek · c. 350 BCE · Turkey
Tomb of the Carian satrap Mausolus, so grand that his name became the word for any lavish tomb. Wonder of the ancient world; shaken down by earthquakes; its fragments in the British Museum.
Apoxyomenos
by Lysippos
Classical Greek · Lysippos · c. 330 BCE
An athlete scraping oil from his arm. The Roman marble copy stands in for a lost bronze original by Alexander the Great's favorite sculptor.
Dying Gaul
Hellenistic · c. 230–220 BCE (Roman copy)
A defeated Celt collapsing on his shield, wound dripping. Pergamene art showed the enemy as human — a compliment paid in marble, after the kill.
Barberini Faun
Hellenistic · c. 220 BCE
A sleeping satyr sprawled drunk on a rock, legs splayed, utterly without dignity. Excavated from Hadrian's mausoleum moat — perhaps thrown over the walls during a siege.
Winged Victory of Samothrace
Hellenistic · c. 200 BCE
Nike landing on the prow of a ship, wind pressing her drapery against her body. One of the most physically dynamic pieces of ancient sculpture.
Pergamon Altar
Hellenistic · c. 180–160 BCE · Pergamon
A colossal marble frieze of the gods fighting the giants — deep-cut writhing bodies that may have inspired Michelangelo. The whole structure was hauled to Berlin in 1880.
House of the Faun
Roman · 2nd c. BCE · Pompeii
One of the largest residences in Pompeii, three acres across. Its atrium held the bronze dancing faun that gave it the name; the floor held the Alexander Mosaic.
Antikythera Mechanism
Hellenistic · c. 150–100 BCE · Greece
A corroded bronze device pulled from a Roman shipwreck in 1901. CT scans finally revealed: a geared orrery predicting sun, moon, and five planets. No surviving match until the Renaissance.
Venus de Milo
Hellenistic Greek · c. 130–100 BCE
Marble Aphrodite found on the island of Milos in 1820. The missing arms are part of her fame — we don't know what she was doing.
Boxer at Rest
Hellenistic · c. 100 BCE
A bronze boxer after a fight — cauliflower ears, bloodied lip, exhausted. One of the few surviving Greek originals, not a later copy.
Alexander Mosaic
Roman copy of Hellenistic painting · c. 100 BCE · Pompeii
Alexander charging Darius at Issus, rendered in a million tiny tesserae. The lost original oil painting was probably by Philoxenos of Eretria.
Borghese Gladiator
by Agasias of Ephesus
c. 100 BCE · Marble · Sully Wing
Not actually a gladiator — a warrior fighting a mounted opponent, arm raised with a (lost) shield. A tour de force of anatomical study; every muscle is taut with the effort of the moment before impact.
Villa of the Mysteries
Roman frescoed villa · c. 60 BCE · Pompeii
Life-size figures on a crimson ground enact what looks like the initiation of a bride into a Dionysian cult — a flagellation, a satyr's mask, a winged figure about to whip a kneeling woman. No one is sure whether the scene is myth, rite, or both.
Laocoön and His Sons
Hellenistic · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE
A Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents. Dug up in a vineyard in 1506 and hauled to the Vatican. Michelangelo studied it for weeks.
Boy with Thorn
Hellenistic → Roman · 1st c. BCE (later copies)
A boy pulling a thorn from his foot, head bent in total concentration. One of the first ancient bronzes to be widely copied in the Renaissance.
Ara Pacis
Augustan Rome · 13–9 BCE
The Altar of Peace. The imperial family processes in carved marble, framed by acanthus tendrils. Augustus inserts himself into the mythic past.

0 – 500 CE

Maison Carrée
Roman temple · c. 2 CE · Nîmes
The best-preserved Roman temple anywhere, built by Agrippa for the imperial cult. Thomas Jefferson obsessed over its proportions and based the Virginia State Capitol on it, launching the Roman revival in American civic architecture.
Portland Vase
Roman cameo glass · c. 1–25 CE
A cobalt-blue glass vase dipped in white and carved back through the outer layer. One of the finest surviving pieces of Roman cameo glass — and inspiration for Wedgwood's first portraits.
Augustus of Prima Porta
Early Roman Empire · c. 15 CE
The emperor posed as a general, barefoot like a god. The breastplate shows the return of captured Roman standards — a diplomatic win dressed as conquest.
Pont du Gard
Roman aqueduct · 1st century CE · southern France
A three-tiered limestone aqueduct carrying water fifty kilometers to Nîmes across the Gardon valley. Each block weighs up to six tonnes and was set without mortar; the gradient over the full course drops only 12.6 meters.
Pula Arena
Roman amphitheater · 1st century CE · Istria, Croatia
One of only six surviving Roman amphitheaters that retain a complete outer wall. Seating 23,000 for gladiatorial combat, the limestone ring still hosts opera and concerts under the open sky each summer.
The Colosseum
Flavian · 70–80 CE · Rome
An elliptical amphitheater for 50,000 spectators. The underground hypogeum ran elevators, flooded the floor for sea battles, and housed wild animals.
Pompeii
Roman · buried 79 CE
Vesuvius sealed a whole Roman town in volcanic ash. Frescoes, bread loaves, graffiti, and the shapes of bodies all survived — daily life frozen.
Herculaneum
Roman · buried 79 CE · Italy
Pompeii's smaller, richer neighbor. Volcanic mud sealed it instead of ash, so wooden beams, baby cradles, and carbonized scrolls all survived.
Arch of Titus
Roman Imperial · 81 CE · Rome
Triumphal arch for the sack of Jerusalem. Its inner relief shows the Menorah and other temple spoils being carried in procession — our earliest image of the Second Temple's contents.
Trajan's Column
Imperial Roman · 113 CE
A 100-foot marble column whose spiral frieze narrates the Dacian Wars in 155 continuous scenes — history carved as a comic strip.
Library of Celsus at Ephesus
Roman Imperial · 110–135 CE · Turkey
Two-story facade of a library that held 12,000 scrolls and the sarcophagus of its founder in a chamber behind the wall — a library as a tomb.
Pantheon
Roman Imperial · 126 CE · Rome
The largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The oculus at its center has been open to the sky for nineteen centuries — rain falls through it into drains in the floor.
Hadrian's Villa
Roman Imperial · 117–138 CE · Tivoli
A 250-acre retreat where the emperor built scale models of the sights from his tour of the empire — a Nile canal, an Athenian stoa, a Canopus.
Apollo Belvedere
Hellenistic / Roman copy · c. 120–140 CE
A marble copy of a lost Greek bronze, long considered the pinnacle of male beauty. Winckelmann helped launch Neoclassicism by writing about this single statue.
Sleeping Hermaphroditus
Roman copy of a Greek original · 2nd century CE · Sully Wing
From one angle, a sleeping woman. Walk around to the other side: the body is both. The mattress beneath — realistic enough that you want to touch it — was carved by Bernini in 1619 when the statue was restored.
Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
Roman Imperial · c. 175 CE
The only surviving large Roman bronze equestrian. It lived because Christians mistook it for Constantine. Now replaced by a copy in the Campidoglio; the original is inside.
Leptis Magna
Roman provincial city · expanded 193 CE · Libya
Hometown of Septimius Severus and the showpiece of Roman Africa, with its four-way arch, Severan basilica, and enormous forum paved in imported marble. Desert sand buried and preserved it after the Arab conquest.
Baths of Caracalla
Roman imperial · 216 CE · Rome
A 25-hectare bathing complex that could hold 1,600 people at once, heated by underfloor hypocausts stoked by armies of slaves. The painted vaults fell centuries ago but Shelley wrote much of Prometheus Unbound wandering the ruins.
Diocletian's Palace
Late Roman · 305 CE · Split, Croatia
A retired emperor's fortified seaside retreat built on the plan of a military camp. Its peristyle, mausoleum (now the cathedral), and substructures form the kernel around which the medieval city of Split grew.
Arch of Constantine
Late Roman · 315 CE · Rome
A triumphal arch cobbled together from reused second-century reliefs of Trajan, Hadrian, and Marcus Aurelius — a frank admission that fourth-century sculptors could no longer match the earlier skill. The recycled figures were even recut with Constantine's head.
←Mesopotamia & Persia Europe→
Images via Wikimedia Commons. Click any image to open it and see its source.
